deck.title: Westminster Decarbonization Plan
deck.subtitle: Value-first electrification pathway for a 7-year hold — calibrated model, expert-adjudicated assumptions, and a capital plan that beats the hurdle.

slides.heading: Cortland Westminster at a glance
slides.sub: Garden-style multifamily community entering a regulated compliance window mid-hold. Figures illustrative.

items: Gas-dominant heating and domestic hot water; major plant equipment reaches remaining useful life inside the hold.
Asset sits above its BPD multifamily peer group on energy intensity — headroom, not a crisis.
Ownership objective: protect exit value and stay ahead of compliance without over-capitalizing a 7-year hold.

slides.heading: Colorado Regulation 28 sets the clock
slides.sub: Building performance standards convert energy intensity into a dated financial liability.

items: Regulation 28 requires covered buildings to hit energy-intensity targets at 2026 and 2030 checkpoints, with penalties for non-compliance.
Business-as-usual trajectory misses the 2030 target — fines land inside the hold, before exit.
Equipment replaced like-for-like at end of life locks in the non-compliant profile for another 15+ years.
The question is not whether to spend at RUL — it is whether the incremental dollars buy compliance and value.

blocks.text: Every dollar in this plan is incremental over spend the asset already owes at end of equipment life.
blocks.attribution: Framing principle for the capital plan

slides.heading: How the plan was built
slides.sub: A calibrated building model, measures timed to equipment life, and value-creation math — with expert-in-the-loop gates at every consequential step.

items: Calibrated energy model: physics-based simulation tuned to 24 months of actual utility data (kWh, calibrated to within acceptance tolerance).
Measures at remaining useful life: interventions scheduled when equipment is due anyway, so only the incremental cost counts.
Value-creation math: incremental cost vs. landlord-share utility savings, avoided fines, incentives, and conditional exit-value impact.
Expert-in-the-loop gates: every material assumption was surfaced, adjudicated, and locked before economics were run.

slides.heading: What counts as value — and what doesn't

items: Incremental capital only — like-for-like replacement cost is netted out.
Landlord-share utility savings, per the owner/tenant meter split.
PV of avoided Reg-28 penalties across both checkpoints.
Conditional exit-value impact, applied only where buyer underwriting supports it.
No tenant-paid savings claimed by the landlord.
No green premium asserted without market evidence.
No incentive counted before program eligibility is confirmed.
All displayed figures rounded to 2 significant figures; benchmarks vs. BPD peers.

slides.sub: Locked inputs used across every scenario. Figures illustrative.

rows: Adjudicated — rent roll vs. assessor records

slides.heading: Questions raised — and where they stand
slides.sub: Every consequential assumption went through an expert-in-the-loop gate before the economics were run.

items.note: Rent roll (504) conflicts with assessor record (512). Adjudicated to 504 — eight units were combined during the 2014 renovation.

items.q: Owner/tenant utility split for in-unit electric
items.note: Tenants are direct-metered for in-unit electric; landlord carries common-area electric and all gas. Savings allocated accordingly.

items.q: Exit cap rate 5.5% — hold or sensitize?
items.note: Held at sponsor guidance; economics shown with and without exit-value impact so the conclusion doesn't hinge on it.
Three months interpolated from degree-day regression. Requesting utility re-pull; affects the compliance margin, not the plan selection.

slides.heading: The plan pays for itself — and clears the hurdle
slides.sub: Unlevered, incremental basis over the 7-year hold. Figures illustrative.

items.label: Fines avoided
items.sub: PV of Reg-28 penalties, both checkpoints

items: Cashflow turns positive in year 4; the exit-value component is conditional and shown separately.
Plan remains hurdle-positive with the exit-value impact fully excluded.

slides.heading: Two credible plans, one recommendation
slides.sub: Plan B is the recommendation: full electrification at RUL, phased across the hold. Figures illustrative.

rows: Plan A + heat-pump space conditioning at furnace RUL

slides.heading: Carbon intensity drops ahead of both curves
slides.sub: Site GHGI under Plan B vs. business-as-usual, against both compliance objects. Figures illustrative.

blocks.title: Carbon intensity — with-plan vs. BAU vs. targets
blocks.note: BPS target drives fines; the CRREM pathway drives stranding — two different objects, both cleared under Plan B. BAU strands in 2031, inside the hold; the with-plan trajectory stays under the CRREM 1.5°C pathway through exit.

slides.heading: Where the $3.9M comes from
slides.sub: Incremental basis: like-for-like replacement cost is netted out before the bridge starts. Figures illustrative.

blocks.title: Plan B value creation — 7-year hold
blocks.annotation: Exit-value impact is conditional on buyer underwriting; the plan remains hurdle-positive with it fully excluded.

slides.heading: Revisions and the path to execution

items: 1. Close the 2021 baseline data gap — utility re-pull requested; compliance margin to be re-stated on receipt.
2. Confirm incentive eligibility (utility DSM + IRA 179D pathway) before locking the year-1 capital budget.
3. Board review of Plan B capital ask at the next investment committee.
4. Commission the year-1 package: DHW heat-pump design and controls scope, aligned to the 2026 checkpoint.
5. Standing revision cadence: plan re-run annually with actuals, and at any material change in tariffs or Reg-28 rulemaking.

blocks.text: This plan is a living document — every revision goes back through the same expert-in-the-loop gates that built it.
